source_paper_title: Generalization Bounds in the Predict-Then-Optimize Framework
source_paper_bibtex: @article{El_Balghiti_2023, title={Generalization Bounds in the Predict-Then-Optimize Framework}, volume={48}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2022.1330}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2022.1330}, number={4}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={El Balghiti, Othman and Elmachtoub, Adam N. and Grigas, Paul and Tewari, Ambuj}, year={2023}, month=nov, pages={2043–2065} }
context:
Page 2, Introduction (discussion following margin-based bounds and contraction lemmas); reiterated on page 8, start of Section 4.2 (margin-based generalization bounds).

Even though we construct a Lipschitz upper bound on SPO loss in a general norm setting (Theorem 3), our margin bounds (Theorem 4) are stated in the \(\ell_2\) norm setting. This is because the most general contraction type lemma for vector valued Lipschitz functions we know of only works for the \(\ell_2\)-norm [17]. Similar results are available in the infinity norm setting [3] but our understanding of general norms appears limited at present.

To the best of our knowledge, extending the vector-contraction inequality of [17] to an arbitrary norm setting (or even the case of general \(\ell_q\)-norms) remains an open question that would have interesting applications to our framework.
title: Extend vector-contraction inequalities for Rademacher complexity to general norms
background_latex:
Let \(\mathcal X\) be an input (feature) space and \(\mathcal H\) be a class of vector-valued hypotheses \(f:\mathcal X\to\mathbb R^d\). Fix an i.i.d. sample \(x_1,\dots,x_n\in\mathcal X\). The (multivariate) empirical Rademacher complexity of \(\mathcal H\) is
\[
\widehat{\mathfrak R}_n(\mathcal H):=\mathbb E_{\sigma}\Big[\sup_{f\in\mathcal H}\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n \sigma_i^\top f(x_i)\Big],
\]
where \(\sigma_1,\dots,\sigma_n\in\{\pm1\}^d\) have i.i.d. Rademacher coordinates and \(\mathbb E_\sigma\) denotes expectation over these signs.

Let \(\|\cdot\|\) be a norm on \(\mathbb R^d\). Consider a real-valued function \(\phi:\mathbb R^d\to\mathbb R\) that is \(L\)-Lipschitz with respect to \(\|\cdot\|\), i.e.,
\[
|\phi(u)-\phi(v)|\le L\,\|u-v\|\quad\text{for all }u,v\in\mathbb R^d.
\]
Define the composed scalar class \(\phi\circ\mathcal H:=\{x\mapsto \phi(f(x)):\ f\in\mathcal H\}\). Its empirical Rademacher complexity is
\[
\widehat{\mathfrak R}_n(\phi\circ\mathcal H):=\mathbb E_{\epsilon}\Big[\sup_{f\in\mathcal H}\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n \epsilon_i\,\phi(f(x_i))\Big],
\]
where \(\epsilon_1,\dots,\epsilon_n\in\{\pm1\}\) are i.i.d. Rademacher signs.

A vector-contraction inequality is a bound that controls \(\widehat{\mathfrak R}_n(\phi\circ\mathcal H)\) in terms of \(L\) and \(\widehat{\mathfrak R}_n(\mathcal H)\) (or a closely related complexity), possibly with a constant depending on the choice of norm \(\|\cdot\|\). Known general results (e.g., Maurer 2016) provide such inequalities in the case \(\|\cdot\|=\|\cdot\|_2\), and some related results exist for \(\|\cdot\|_\infty\), but general norms (including general \(\ell_q\) norms) are not covered by the currently available contraction tools referenced by the paper.
problem_definition_latex:
Develop a vector-contraction inequality for empirical Rademacher complexity for Lipschitz maps under a general norm \(\|\cdot\|\) on \(\mathbb R^d\): find conditions and an explicit bound of the form
\[
\widehat{\mathfrak R}_n(\phi\circ\mathcal H)\le C(\|\cdot\|)\,L\,\widehat{\mathfrak R}_n(\mathcal H)
\]
(or an analogous bound in terms of a suitable multivariate Rademacher complexity), where \(\phi:\mathbb R^d\to\mathbb R\) is \(L\)-Lipschitz with respect to \(\|\cdot\|\), \(\mathcal H\subseteq\{f:\mathcal X\to\mathbb R^d\}\), and \(C(\|\cdot\|)\) is an explicit constant or function of the norm, extending the known \(\ell_2\)-based vector-contraction inequality to general norms (including general \(\ell_q\) norms).

\[

location_in_paper: Page 2, Introduction (discussion following margin-based bounds and contraction lemmas); reiterated on page 8, start of Section 4.2 (margin-based generalization bounds).
open_problem_quote:
Even though we construct a Lipschitz upper bound on SPO loss in a general norm setting (Theorem 3), our margin bounds (Theorem 4) are stated in the \(\ell_2\) norm setting. This is because the most general contraction type lemma for vector valued Lipschitz functions we know of only works for the \(\ell_2\)-norm [17]. Similar results are available in the infinity norm setting [3] but our understanding of general norms appears limited at present.

To the best of our knowledge, extending the vector-contraction inequality of [17] to an arbitrary norm setting (or even the case of general \(\ell_q\)-norms) remains an open question that would have interesting applications to our framework.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The problem asks for a general inequality holding uniformly over function classes and Lipschitz maps, rather than for existence/nonexistence on a finite instance family. Automated search might find toy counterexamples for candidate bounds, but the relevant quantifiers and norm-dependent constants make systematic finite search unlikely to be decisive.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: A coding-agent loop could explore candidate inequalities by proposing norm-dependent constants and testing them empirically on randomized finite-dimensional hypothesis classes and Lipschitz functions to search for violations or plausible sharp constants. However, the ultimate goal is a universal proof (or a rigorous counterexample construction), so computational feedback would be only a heuristic guide.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.
